main_damage_causes: Complete absence from GoF and OotP films eliminates the foundational scenes that establish Bill's character, his relationship with Harry, his Order role, and his central arc moment (Greyback attack/hospital wing), leaving DH appearances without emotional grounding

Loss of the GoF hospital wing scene ('I'll go to Dad') removes Bill's single most plot-functional moment — the act that initiates the reconstituted Order — reducing him from an agent of the resistance to a background family member

The HBP omission of Bill's attack, scarring, and the Fleur declaration scene destroys his personal arc and the thematic parallel to Lupin/Tonks, making his scarred appearance in DH unexplained and his relationship with Fleur emotionally unearned for film audiences

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 1 - Seven Potters / Privet Drive departure
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill is introduced in GoF as a fully realized character: cool, long-haired, earring, dragon-hide boots, rock-concert clothes. His first appearance is a warm, confident handshake with Harry and easy banter. By DH he is a known quantity with established presence at the Burrow, Shell Cottage operations, and Order work.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill appears briefly in the crowd, scarred face noted by Fred/George ('Dead ugly'), and he introduces himself to Harry with 'Hello, Harry. Bill Weasley.' as if they have never met. The film treats this as a first introduction despite Harry knowing Bill since GoF.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The film collapses years of established relationship into a cold first-meeting handshake. The warmth, history, and family-elder dynamic Bill has with Harry is erased. The GoF visit to Hogwarts, the Burrow summers, the hospital wing vigil after the Third Task — all rendered invisible, making Bill feel like a stranger rather than a trusted near-family member.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 1 - Burrow aftermath / Mad-Eye announcement
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: After returning from the Seven Potters flight, Bill takes quiet command: he pours firewhisky, sends glasses to everyone with a wand flourish, proposes the toast 'Mad-Eye,' then immediately begins analytical reasoning about the betrayal question, demonstrating calm leadership under grief. He then volunteers without hesitation to go recover Mad-Eye's body.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill delivers the news 'Mad-Eye's dead' and 'Mundungus took one look at Voldemort and Disapparated' flatly, then recedes. The toast, the wand flourish, the analytical speech about why Mundungus couldn't have been the traitor, and the volunteering to recover the body are all cut. His voice breaks in the book ('Bill's voice broke') — emotional texture entirely absent.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book uses this scene to show Bill as the composed, capable eldest son who steps up in crisis — grief-stricken but functional, analytical, and brave enough to go back out into danger. The film reduces him to a news-delivery mechanism. His leadership function and emotional depth in the worst moment of the night are transferred to silence.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 1 - Shell Cottage goblin warning scene
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill pulls Harry aside privately after Lupin's baby announcement, uses the festive moment as cover, and delivers a nuanced, expert warning about goblin notions of ownership — drawing on years of professional experience at Gringotts. He is perceptive enough to have deduced Harry is planning something with Griphook without being told. The scene establishes him as wise, worldly, and genuinely trying to protect Harry while respecting his autonomy.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: The warning is compressed into a brief hallway exchange: 'I don't know what you're up to, but I know goblins. If you've struck any kind of bargain with Griphook, you must be exceptionally careful to live up to it. If you don't... he won't be forgiving.' No private moment, no depth about goblin philosophy of ownership, no demonstration of professional expertise, no emotional complexity.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The book scene is one of Bill's finest moments — it shows his intelligence, his professional expertise, his perceptiveness, and his care for Harry. The film reduces it to a one-line caution that could have been delivered by any character. The loss of the goblin-ownership philosophy also damages the thematic setup for why Harry's promise to Griphook becomes morally fraught.

damaging_scenes.scene: DH Part 1 - Shell Cottage management / family evacuation
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill is the operational hub of Shell Cottage: he manages the overcrowded safe house, moves the Weasley family to Muriel's under Fidelius, acts as Secret-Keeper for the cottage, escorts Ollivander to safety, monitors Griphook, lends Harry a tent, and keeps watch over the trio's planning with quiet concern. He is shown as the de facto head of the resistance cell.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill is present at Shell Cottage but his operational role is largely invisible. The family evacuation, the Fidelius arrangements, the Ollivander escort, and his role as Secret-Keeper are either cut or mentioned only in passing dialogue. He functions as a background presence rather than an active organizer.
damaging_scenes.explanation: A significant portion of Bill's DH function — as the competent, quietly heroic eldest son holding the resistance together from Shell Cottage — is invisible in the film. Audiences have no sense of the cost he is bearing (can't work, family in hiding, cottage overrun) or the leadership he is providing.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF - Hospital wing vigil / 'I'll go to Dad'
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: After Fudge refuses to believe Voldemort has returned, Bill immediately stands up and says 'I'll go to Dad' — volunteering to act as the link between Dumbledore and Arthur Weasley at the Ministry. He then claps Harry on the shoulder, kisses his mother, and strides out. It is a moment of decisive, brave action at a critical juncture.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This entire scene — Bill's vigil in the hospital wing, his presence during the Fudge confrontation, and his decisive action — does not exist in the films. GoF ends without Bill appearing at all.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is Bill's single most important plot-functional moment in the entire saga. His decision to go to Arthur is the first act of the reconstituted Order of the Phoenix. Losing it means the audience never sees Bill as an agent of the resistance, only as a background family member. His courage and decisiveness at the saga's turning point are entirely absent.

damaging_scenes.scene: GoF - Hogwarts visit / morning walk with Harry
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill and Mrs. Weasley attend the Third Task as Harry's family. Bill spends a morning walking the grounds with Harry, reminiscing about Hogwarts ('Haven't seen this place for five years'), asking about Sir Cadogan and the Fat Lady, giving Harry a sense of being cared for. He is warm, curious, and treats Harry as a genuine family member.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill does not appear in the GoF film at all. The Third Task family visit is cut entirely.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The GoF visit is the foundation of Bill and Harry's personal relationship. Without it, their interactions in DH lack emotional grounding. The audience has no basis for understanding why Harry trusts Bill, why Bill's warning carries weight, or why Bill's presence at Shell Cottage feels like safety rather than obligation.

damaging_scenes.scene: HBP - Bill's attack by Greyback / hospital wing / Fleur's declaration
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill is savaged by Greyback during the Death Eater attack on Hogwarts. The hospital wing scene is one of the most emotionally rich in the series: Mrs. Weasley's grief, Fleur's fierce declaration of love, the reconciliation between the two women, Tonks using it as a mirror for her own situation with Lupin. Bill's scarred face and wolfish tendencies afterward are established.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: This scene does not occur in the HBP film. Bill does not appear in HBP at all. His attack, his scarring, and the entire hospital wing emotional climax are absent. His scars are simply present in DH with a brief retroactive explanation.
damaging_scenes.explanation: This is the most structurally damaging omission. The Greyback attack and its aftermath is Bill's central arc moment — it establishes his scars, his wolfish traits, his relationship with Fleur as genuinely tested and triumphant, and provides the thematic parallel to Lupin/Tonks. Without it, Bill's scarred appearance in DH is unexplained to casual viewers, Fleur's devotion lacks its earned emotional payoff, and the Lupin/Tonks subplot loses its mirror. The entire HBP emotional climax around Bill is simply gone.

damaging_scenes.scene: OotP - Grimmauld Place / Order meeting introduction
damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill is working a desk job in London specifically to be near the Order. He is shown in serious discussion with Mr. Weasley and Lupin about goblin politics — demonstrating professional expertise and strategic thinking. He hastily rolls up secret parchments when Harry arrives, showing he is trusted with sensitive Order intelligence.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill does not appear in the OotP film.
damaging_scenes.explanation: OotP establishes Bill as an active, committed Order member with professional expertise in goblin relations — directly relevant to DH. His absence means the audience has no context for why he is trusted with Shell Cottage operations or why his goblin warning to Harry carries authority.

damaging_scenes.book_baseline: Bill arrives at Hogwarts with Fleur as part of the assembled Order and allies. He is present in the Room of Requirement, gently but firmly tells Ginny she cannot fight ('You can't do this. Everyone underage will have to leave, it's only right'), and fights in the battle. He is part of the family group around Fred's body at the end.
damaging_scenes.film_portrayal: Bill is listed among those who emerge in the Great Hall and is briefly visible in the battle, but his specific action of addressing Ginny about staying is cut. His presence is largely that of a background figure in crowd shots.
damaging_scenes.explanation: The Ginny moment is a small but characteristic beat — Bill as the responsible eldest, balancing love for his sister with hard necessity. Its absence is minor but consistent with the film's pattern of reducing Bill to a visual presence rather than a character with voice and judgment.
